V.01 - update 10.02.2026

1.0 CORE CONCEPTS

This section of the core rules contains the essential framework that is used in all games of StarCraft: The Miniatures Game.

1.1 MODELS

The Archon Studio miniatures that make up your army are models. Each model is any single entity (be it a Marine, a Zergling or a massive Ultralisk) mounted on its own independent base. While the model itself represents the character or vehicle, the following rules strictly govern the model via its base.

1.2 UNITS

An army is composed of Units. A Unit consists of one or more models that act together as a single cohesive formation.


  • Composition: The number of models in a Unit is defined by its Unit Card. For example, a basic infantry Unit might consist of several soldiers, while a heavy siege vehicle might operate as a Unit of one.

  • Cohesion: Models within a Unit fight, move, and die collectively. They are bound by the rules of Coherency (section 1.6.4), representing the discipline and communication required to operate on a battlefield.

  • Friendly & Enemy: Units in your army are referred to as friendly Units. Likewise, Units in your opponent’s army are referred to as enemy Units.

1.3 BASES

Every model must be mounted on a base of the correct size, as specified on its Unit Card.


  • The Golden Rule of Bases: In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, the base represents the model's solid physical mass and footprint.

  • No Overlap: Bases may never overlap or stack. If models cannot physically fit into a space without their bases overlapping, they cannot move there.

  • Scenic Elements: Players are encouraged to embrace the hobby aspect of the game. You are free to add scenic basing, elaborate conversions, flight stands, or dramatic poses to your miniatures. However, for the purposes of rules interactions, measurements, and Line of Sight, only the Base and the Size Characteristic listed on the Unit Card matter (the physical height of the miniature is ignored to ensure fair play).

1.4 THE SCALE OF WAR

While the specific rules for building an army (using Minerals and Vespene Gas) will be covered in later sections, it is important to understand the scale of the engagements you will fight. The game is played on a tabletop battlefield, typically populated with terrain pieces like ruined outposts or alien hives.


The size of the battle is defined by the Engagement Scale (measured in Minerals), ranging from:


  • Skirmish: For small, elite patrols and learning games played on smaller battlefields (36" x 36").

  • Standard Engagement: The definitive StarCraft experience, balancing tactical movement with army composition on a standard battlefield (36" x 54").

  • Grand Offensive: Massive, multi-front wars requiring larger battlefields and experienced commanders.


Regardless of the scale, the goal remains the same: use all of the tools at your disposal to crush your enemies and secure the sector.

1.5 THE TOOLS OF WAR

To play games of StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, you will need some six-sided dice and a range ruler or tape measure marked in inches (“).

1.5.1 DICE

The game uses standard six-sided dice, referred to as D6.


  • D6: If a rule requires a D6, roll one die. The result is the number shown on the face.

  • D3: If a rule requires a D3, roll one D6 and halve the result, rounding up:

  • Roll 1–2 = 1

  • Roll 3–4 = 2

  • Roll 5–6 = 3

  • Multiple Dice (2D6, 3D6): If a rule requires, for example, 2D6, roll two dice and add the results together.

1.5.2 ROLL-OFFS

During play, there will be rules that instruct the players to ‘Roll-off’ (such as determining who sets up terrain or deciding who the First Player is) to do so:


  • Both players roll 2D6.

  • The player with the higher total wins.

  • If the totals are equal, roll again until a winner is determined.

1.5.3 RE-ROLLS

Some rules may have instructions for mandatory or optional re-rolls. To re-roll a dice, use the same dice used for the original roll again. The same dice cannot be re-rolled more than once.

1.5.4 MODIFIERS

In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, Modifiers change the Target Number of a dice roll. When a rule grants a Modifier (written as ‘+X’ or ‘-X’), apply it before rolling the dice:


  • ‘+X’ Modifier: This makes the roll easier. You reduce the Target Number of the roll by X.

  • ‘-X’ Modifier: This makes the roll harder. You increase the Target Number of the roll by X.

EXAMPLE

A Marine needs a 5+ to hit a target:

  • If he gains a +1 Modifier. The Target Number is reduced by 1. He now hits on a 4+.

  • He suffers a -1 Modifier. The Target Number is increased by 1. He now hits on a 6+.

1.5.5 FIXED ADDITIONS VS. MODIFIERS

If a rule says "D3+1", this is not a modifier to a Target Number. It instructs you to roll the die and add 1 to the final result (e.g. generating 4 damage instead of 3).

1.5.6 AUTOMATIC SUCCESS AND FAILURE

Regardless of how many modifiers apply:


  • An unmodified roll of 6 is always a Success.

  • An unmodified roll of 1 is always a Failure.

1.5.7 CHARACTERISTIC AND ATTRIBUTE TESTS

Whenever a rule instructs a player to make a Roll, it falls into one of two categories based on the source of the number being tested:


  1. Characteristic Test: A test that uses a value found on the Unit’s Characteristic Profile (e.g. checking Armour or Evade).

  2. Attribute Test: A test that uses a value printed on a Weapon Profile or within a Special Rule (e.g. checking a Weapon's Hit value).


Value Generation: Rolls made to determine a distance or quantity (such as a Charge Roll or D3 Damage) are not Tests. These are resolved by adding the die result to the base value (see 1.5.5 Fixed Additions).


TEST RESOLUTION SEQUENCE 

To resolve either type of test:

  • Identify the stated value (the base Target Number).

  • Apply any Modifiers (see above) to determine the final Target Number.

  • Roll the required die.

  • Success: The roll result is equal to or higher than the final Target Number. 

  • Failure: The roll result is lower than the final Target Number.

1.5.8 COCKED DICE

If a die lands at an angle (cocked) or falls off the table, the roll is void. Re-roll that die immediately. Players should agree before the game begins on what constitutes a "cocked" die to avoid disputes during critical moments.
Click the arrow below to continue to the next page >

1.6 MEASURING AND MOVEMENT

In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, all distances are measured in Inches (") using a tape measure or range ruler. You may measure distances at any time to check ranges and potential move paths before declaring an action.


1.6.1 MEASURING DISTANCES

When checking the distance between two objects on the battlefield:

  • Measuring Between Models: Measure from the closest point of the acting model’s base to the closest point of the target model’s base (shown in the image below).

  • Ignore Overhang: Weapons, wings, limbs, and dynamic scenic elements extending beyond the rim of a model’s base are ignored. Only the base matters.

  • Base-to-Base Contact: If two bases are physically touching, the distance between them is 0".

  • Tokens and Markers: Measure to the closest physical edge of the Token or Marker.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382036636_2.jpg?alt=media&token=fe860e54-b964-4639-9580-b5de143398c0

1.6.2 WITHIN VS. WHOLLY WITHIN

Many rules require a model to be inside an aura, a zone, or range of a Mission Marker. The distinction between "Within" and "Wholly Within" is critical.


WITHIN

A model is Within a distance if any part of its base touches or crosses into the specified range. A Unit is Within a distance if at least one model’s base from that Unit touches or crosses into the specified range.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382096021_3.jpg?alt=media&token=37a21170-5baa-4b52-a8fd-785f01567005

WHOLLY WITHIN

A model is Wholly Within a distance only if its entire base is inside the specified range. No part of the model’s base may hang over the edge. A Unit is Wholly Within a distance only if every single model’s base in that Unit is inside the specified range. No part of any model’s base from that Unit may hang over the edge.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382153659_4.jpg?alt=media&token=790b602f-39a0-4331-a8db-00de120e4fd0

1.6.3 THE LEADING MODEL 

The Leading Model is a temporary reference point used to execute the movement of a Unit. Whenever a Unit resolves a Move, Run, Charge, Disengage, or Close Ranks action, you must nominate one model from that Unit to be the Leading Model.


Move the Leading Model first, measuring its path exactly to ensure the base physically fits at every point along that path. Then, place the rest of the Unit in valid Coherency relative to its new position. The nomination ends as soon as the action is resolved.

1.6.4 UNIT COHERENCY

Each Unit acts as a single disciplined formation. To remain combat-effective, every model in a Unit must satisfy two conditions regarding the Unit's Leading Model.


PROXIMITY

Every model in the Unit must be Wholly Within 3" horizontally and Within 5" vertically of the Leading Model.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382255511_5.jpg?alt=media&token=bbf4d70c-f7ba-4d61-9cb8-66ad9e941249

COHERENCY LINK

When a rule tells you to move or place a Unit, the controlling player moves or places the Leading Model first, then places the remaining models one at a time. Each model should be placed Wholly Within 3" of the Leading Model and able to trace a Coherency Link to it through a continuous chain of imaginary lines drawn model-to-model. Coherency Links cannot pass through enemy models or terrain the Leading Model cannot move through. Any model unable to find a position that maintains the Coherency Link is immediately removed as a casualty.


UNIT IN COHERENCY

A Unit is In Coherency if all of its models are Wholly Within 3” of its Leading Model and Coherency Links can be drawn from each other model in the Unit to the Leading Model.


Models unable to fit Wholly Within 3” must be placed as close as possible to the Leading Model while still maintaining the Coherency Link. 


OUT OF COHERENCY

If, at any time, a model from a Unit is not Wholly Within 3” of its Leading Model, the Unit is Out of Coherency.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382293511_6.jpg?alt=media&token=031ad168-31ce-4b73-9c6f-6db7523c1c07
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382337263_7.jpg?alt=media&token=ad549410-65bf-4fad-a763-610cab8efa6c

1.6.5 DIRECTLY TOWARDS & DIRECTLY AWAY

Some abilities may force a unit to move directly towards or directly away from another unit on the battlefield. When this is the case, the path of this move is determined by drawing an imaginary straight line from the center of the acting model’s base through the center of the targeted model’s base.  


The affected model must adhere to this line when moving in a straight line directly towards or directly away from the target model. However, if the path of the imaginary line is blocked by terrain or another model, the affected model must bypass them via the shortest possible route to continue in the direction specified. If there are two valid equidistant paths, the controlling player of the affected model may choose which one the affected model will take.

When moving directly towards, and the affected Unit contains more than one model, the Leading Model must be the model physically closest to the target the Unit is moving towards. Move the Leading Model first. The remaining models must then be Placed as close to the target as possible, while maintaining Unit Coherency. Models that cannot end a movement closer to the target cannot move at all. If the target Unit contains more than one model, the imaginary line must be drawn towards the center of the base of the model physically closest to the acting model.

When moving directly away, and the affected Unit contains more than one model, the Leading Model must be the model physically farthest from the target the Unit is moving away from. Move the Leading Model first. The remaining models must then be Placed as far from the target as possible, while maintaining Unit Coherency. Models that cannot end a movement farther from the target cannot move at all. If the target Unit contains more than one model, the imaginary line must be drawn towards the center of the base of the model physically farthest from the acting model.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770382389756_8.jpg?alt=media&token=c2ee887a-aaeb-4da1-82c0-61de0deb8ad1

1.7 UNIT CARDS AND STATISTICS

Every Unit in StarCraft: The Miniatures Game has a Unit Card, which details all of the important information required to use it in a game. Each Unit Card is divided into sections as follows:
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770392026875_10a%20.JPG?alt=media&token=37c8aef1-5b81-4c5b-85f3-221a929ed614

1. Unit Name

  2. Speed: The maximum distance in inches a Leading Model can move during a standard Move action.

    1. Split Values: If two values are shown (e.g. 4"/7"), the second value is used only when the Unit is reduced to a single remaining model or if a unit has a single starting model.

  3. Evade: A secondary defensive characteristic representing agility or dodging. Used only when a specific rule or condition grants an Evade Roll.

  4. Armour: The Unit's means of resisting Damage. This is the Target Number required to save against a successful hit.

  5. Hit Points (HP): The amount of Damage a single model must suffer to be removed from the battlefield.

    1. Shields: Some Units may have a Shields value, this number is added to the Hit Points of the first model removed from the Unit. Once the first model dies, the Shield bonus is lost for the remainder of the Unit.

  6. Size: The vertical height category of the model (e.g. Size 1, Size 2), used for Line of Sight and Terrain interaction.

  7. Supply Profile: This chart shows the Unit's Supply Value based on the number of models remaining in the Unit on the battlefield.

    1. Dynamic Updates: As the Unit takes casualties, you must check this chart. If the model count drops into a lower bracket, the Unit's Supply Value changes immediately.

    2. These values are used for Deployment costs, Mission Marker Control, and Disengage checks.

  8. Phases Boxes: Unique rules to be used during the dedicated Phase.

  9. Weapon Profiles: Each Unit card lists the weapons available to the Unit. Each profile contains:

    1. [RNG] Range: The maximum effective distance in inches.

    2. Melee: Listed as E (Engagement Range) 

    3. Templates: Listed with FT (Flamer Template) or BT (Blast Template).

    4. Target: Restrictions on what the weapon can hit (e.g. GROUND only or FLYING only).

    5. [RoA] Rate of Attack: The number of dice rolled per model firing this weapon.

    6. Hit: The Target Number required on a D6 to score a hit.

    7. Surge Type: The specific Enemy Combat Tag (e.g. Light, Armoured) that triggers the weapon’s Surge efficiency.

    8. S Dice (Surge Dice): The type of die (D3, D6) rolled to determine Surge results.

    9. [DMG] Damage: The amount of damage inflicted by a single unsaved hit.

    10. Keywords: Special rules applying to this weapon (e.g. PIERCE, BLAST, SIDEARM).

  10. Tags: The Unit’s Combat Tags.

  11. Faction Tag: The Faction the Unit belongs to.

1.7.1 SPECIAL RULES AND UPGRADES

The reverse side of a Unit card lists optional equipment that can be purchased during Army Building.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770392861650_11ccc.JPG?alt=media&token=a99591d2-e4e2-4d53-909c-fa823db7d038

1.7.1 SPECIAL RULES AND UPGRADES

The reverse side of a Unit card lists optional equipment that can be purchased during Army Building.

  1. Upgrades: New abilities or weapons added to the profile.

  2. Replacements (↑ FOR): If an upgrade lists "↑ FOR [Weapon Name]", the new weapon replaces the original weapon on the Unit’s profile.

  3. Null Value (-): The Unit lacks this capability entirely.

  4. Army Slot: The Army Slot required to be available to field the unit.

  5. Fluffy description of the unit efficiency in the game. 

  6. The diameter of the base on which the model must be mounted
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770392345717_10cc.JPG?alt=media&token=1ed5fc46-2908-48ec-90ce-4b5ea337a397
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770393087534_111ccc.JPG?alt=media&token=de681ec8-7190-41eb-a14f-40fa23b21635

1.8 KEYWORDS AND RULE PRIORITY

StarCraft: The Miniatures Game is a game of exceptions. To resolve conflicts between text sources, apply the following hierarchy of authority:


Specific Rules → General Rules: Specific rules supersede general rules. If the text on a Unit Card, Mission Card, or Special Ability contradicts the core rules, the specific card or ability takes precedence.

1.8.1 KEYWORDS

Keywords act as shorthand for complex rules. They are written in BOLD CAPS (e.g. SIDEARM, PLACE, PIERCE). A Keyword always means the same thing, regardless of which Unit has it. The definitions are found in the Keyword Glossary (Section 5.4).

Duplicate Keywords: Gaining HIDDEN twice provides no extra benefit.

Numeric Values (X): If a Unit has PRECISION (1) and gains PRECISION (2), you do not add them. You use only the single instance with the highest value. Keywords do not stack.

1.8.2 ABILITY TYPES

Special abilities define a faction's playstyle. They are categorized into three strict types:


  1. Active Abilities: These require a player to voluntarily trigger them.

    1. Timing: Used during the Unit's Activation (or as specified).

    2. Reserves: Cannot be used while the Unit is in Reserves unless explicitly stated.

    3. Limit: A specific Unit may use a named Active Ability only once per Round.

      1. REPETITIVE: If marked REPETITIVE, it can be used multiple times.


  1. Passive Abilities: These represent a Unit’s inherent abilities.

    1. Effect: Always active while the Unit is on the battlefield.

    2. Reserves: Inactive while in Reserves.


  1. Reaction Abilities: These break the standard turn sequence.

    1. Trigger: Used in response to a specific event (e.g. "When targeted by an attack").

    2. Timing: Must be used at the exact moment triggered.

    3. The Reaction Limit: A player may resolve only one Reaction per each Activation. 

    4. Priority: If both players want to react to the same event, the Active Player resolves theirs first.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384376852_add%20card%20exaMPLE%201.jpg?alt=media&token=755c1d6b-754a-460f-9e13-985d351ec33d

https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384393713_add%20card%20exaMPLE%201%20%E2%80%94%20kopia.jpg?alt=media&token=4abf331b-6691-4734-82f3-bed404387aa7

2.0 CORE MECHANICS

This section of the core rules covers the Supply system, Line of Sight, Terrain and how to use the game’s tokens and markers. 

2.1 THE SUPPLY SYSTEM

In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, Supply represents a Unit’s battlefield presence - its manpower, momentum, and the command resources it demands to keep fighting. Unlike fixed characteristics such as Speed or Armour, Supply is dynamic. As a Unit takes losses, its Supply drops, reflecting a force that is running out of bodies, ammo, and cohesion.

2.1.1 THE SUPPLY PROFILE

Every Unit Card features a Supply Profile in the top-right corner. This profile links the number of remaining models to a Current Supply Value. As soon as a Unit’s model count drops into a lower bracket due to casualties, its Current Supply Value changes immediately.


EXAMPLE

A squad of 9 Marines has a Supply of 2. If reduced to 2 models, their profile might indicate their Supply drops to 0.

2.2 SUPPLY

Supply is used to resolve these critical game functions:

DEPLOYMENT

Players cannot deploy their entire army onto the field instantly.

  1. Supply Pool: Each mission grants a specific Supply Pool that caps the total Supply allowed on the battlefield. This pool may increase each round.

  2. Available Supply: You may only deploy reinforcements from Reserves if their Supply Cost fits within your Available Supply (Supply Pool minus Total Supply currently on the battlefield).

  3. Hard Cap: At no point can the Total Current Supply of your Units on the battlefield exceed your Supply Pool.


MISSION MARKER CONTROL

To succeed on the battlefield, Mission Markers must be secured.

  1. Check: When checking control, do not count models. Instead, sum the Current Supply Value of all eligible Units within 3" of the marker.

  2. The Winner: The player with the highest total Supply controls the Mission Marker.

  3. Supply ‘0’ Units: A Unit reduced to Supply 0 (via casualties) can control a marker if there are no enemy Units  contesting it.

  4. Out of Coherency: Regardless of the Units' Current Supply Value it cannot control Mission Markers if it is Out of Coherency (Section1.6.4).


TACTICAL MASS

Supply represents momentum in close quarters. Normally, a Unit that Disengages (retreats from melee) suffers a penalty (it cannot Shoot or Charge next Phase). However, if the Disengaging Unit’s Current Supply is higher than the Combined Supply of all enemies it is Engaged with, it ignores this penalty.

UNIT STARTING SUPPLY

Some Mission Cards allow you to score Victory Points equal to the enemy Supply that was destroyed in a round. In these instances, the Supply Value reduced during that round is added to score during the Scoring Phase.

2.3 LINE OF SIGHT AND TERRAIN

StarCraft: The Miniatures Game uses a consistent 2D Line of Sight (LoS) system. To determine if an acting model can see a target. Trace an imaginary straight line from any part of the acting model’s base to any part of the target model’s base. If this line is not obstructed by Blocking Terrain, the target is Visible.

2.3.1 DETERMINING TERRAIN SIZE

Before the game begins, players should agree on the Size value of each terrain piece. If a strict standard is required, assign Size based on the physical height of the object:

  • Playmat : GROUND LEVEL

  • Under 0.5" : Size 0 (Scatter Terrain / Debris)

  • 0.5" – 1.5" : Size 1 (Low Walls / Barricades / Bushes / Ramps)

  • 1.51" – 3.0" : Size 2 (Standard Ruins / Structures)

  • 3.01" – 4.0" : Size 3 (Large Structures / Tall Walls / HIGH GROUND)

  • Over 4.0" : Size = Height in Inches (rounded down, e.g., 5.2" = Size 5)
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384657520_11.jpg?alt=media&token=9616e32e-c4cd-4716-8c78-3a64573de284

2.3.2 BLOCKING TERRAIN (SIZE VS. EFFECTIVE SIZE)

Not all terrain blocks sight. A low wall hides a Zergling but does nothing to hide a Colossus. A terrain piece blocks LoS only if it meets BOTH of the following conditions:


  1. Blocks the View (Attacker Check): The terrain’s Effective Size (Section 2.3.4) must be equal to or larger than the Attacker’s Effective Size. The wall must be tall enough relative to the shooter to obstruct their view.

  2. Hides the Target (Target Check): The terrain’s Size must be equal to or larger than the Target’s Effective Size. The wall must be physically tall enough to obscure the target model.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384719334_12.jpg?alt=media&token=2dabd355-1bc7-44d2-a0bc-06bc28418505
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384742704_13.jpg?alt=media&token=c3959b6a-ac7e-4384-a161-08438bf1d51a

2.3.3 TERRAIN MASKING

Line of Sight is blocked if a line passes through a terrain piece and the model (Attacker or Target) is Within 1" of that terrain piece, provided the terrain is of the same Size or larger than the model. If BOTH the Attacker and the Target are Within 1" of the same terrain piece (e.g. fighting on opposite sides of the same wall), ignore this rule and use the standard LoS rules.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384804963_14.jpg?alt=media&token=cc85b379-5a0e-4882-a66b-40f1f05561e1

2.3.4 VERTICALITY AND EFFECTIVE SIZE

Standard elevated platforms have the HIGH GROUND keyword and generally measure Over 4.0" in height. When a model stands on top of a terrain piece (like HIGH GROUND) its Effective Size = Model Size + Terrain Size. 


EXAMPLE

A Marine (Size 2) on HIGH GROUND (Size 4) has an Effective Size of 6. He towers over the battlefield, seeing over Size 5 walls that would normally blind him on the ground.


TOP-DOWN PERSPECTIVE

When checking LoS involving elevation, view the battlefield from directly above (2D plane). Only vertical terrain between the two models blocks the shot; the floor they stand on does not.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384858294_15.jpg?alt=media&token=5b843f9e-fb4e-4e25-9bd2-5379f78734a4

STACKING TERRAIN

Terrain can be placed on other terrain. If a terrain piece that normally has its own Size is placed on HIGH GROUND, its Effective Size is increased in the same way as if it were a regular model (it gains Effective Size from the Size of the terrain it is standing on).
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770384904137_16.jpg?alt=media&token=76d7a016-365f-45c8-b46b-333e41d606a4

2.4 MARKERS AND TOKENS

StarCraft: The Miniatures Game uses different markers and tokens to keep the battlefield readable. They are used to distinguish between physical objects and abstract reminders. 

2.4.1 TOKENS

Tokens represent tangible assets, fields, or hazards (e.g. Creep Tumors, Forcefields, Auto-Turrets). Tokens are treated as Terrain (Size 0) with their own base. Models can move through them but cannot end movement overlapping them unless specified. Tokens are removed at the end of the Game Round, unless stated otherwise. Always measure to the closest base edge of a Token.

2.4.2 MARKERS

Markers track different statuses and game states. They have no physical presence and do not block movement or LoS, and come in five different types:


  1. Mission Markers

    1. Numbered Mission Markers placed during Setup.

    2. Affinity: Markers 1/3 are Red; Markers 2/4 are Blue; Marker 5 is Neutral.

    3. States: Double-sided (Activated/Deactivated) to track mission parameters.

    4. Size: 32mm in diameter


  1. Activation Markers

    1. Placed next to a Unit to show it has acted.

    2. Activation Markers are Double-Sided:

      1. Arrow Side: Unit has acted in Phase 1 (Movement).

      2. Reverse Side: Unit has acted in Phase 2 (Assault).


  1. Faction Indicators

Used to mark control of Mission Markers or other game effects.


  1. Zone of Influence Markers

Zone of Influence Markers are L-shaped, faction-themed corner markers used to define the boundaries of a player’s Zone of Influence when an ENTRY EDGE does not cover the full length of a battlefield edge. When a Deployment Card restricts an ENTRY EDGE to a specific section, place Zone of Influence Markers to show the outer limits of that zone exactly as instructed by the Deployment Card.


  1. Status Markers

    1. Plastic status markers are used to track modes like Siege Mode or Burrowed.

    2. Paper status markers are used to track temporary stat changes (Blue = Buff, Red = Debuff).
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770399268706_41.jpg?alt=media&token=250d335a-242e-4478-b204-fc554016073a

2.5 DESTROYED UNITS

If every model from a Unit is removed from the battlefield, that Unit is Destroyed. All effects, abilities and conditions currently associated with that Unit immediately cease and are no longer in play.

2.6 ENGAGEMENT AND ENGAGEMENT RANGE

Engagement Range is the lethal zone where models are close enough to strike in close-quarters combat. It defines when a Unit is "locked" in battle and restricts movement and shooting. Engagement Range extends 1" horizontally from a model’s base. If two enemy models are within Engagement Range of each other, they are considered Engaged.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385019405_18.jpg?alt=media&token=e260ea47-f3de-4fb4-be83-55869681fca6

2.6.1 RESTRICTIONS ON ENGAGEMENT 

Models are only considered Engaged if they meet the following criteria:


  1. Combat Tags: Models can only Engage enemies that operate in the same physical space.

    1. GROUND models only Engage GROUND models.

    2. FLYING models only Engage FLYING models.


  1. Terrain Barriers: Terrain pieces can physically block models from fighting.

    1. Permissible Terrain (Size 0-1): Models can be Engaged across a terrain piece if it is Size 0 or Size 1 (e.g. barricades, low walls).

    2. Blocking Terrain (Size 2+): Models cannot be Engaged through a terrain piece of Size 2 or larger. If a high wall separates two models, they are not Engaged, even if they are within 1" of each other.

    3. Models on HIGH GROUND cannot be engaged with models on GROUND LEVEL, and vice versa.

    4. Models on RAMP LEVEL can be engaged with models on HIGH GROUND and GROUND LEVEL.

3.0 PREPARING FOR BATTLE

This section of the core rules describes how to build an army of Archon Studio miniatures, how to pick which mission will be played, setting up the battlefield and how to deploy your army.

3.1 ARMY BUILDING

To build an army in StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, players must first manage Minerals and Vespene Gas to determine the Engagement Scale of the game being played, which factions they will use, which Units they will bring, what tactical cards they will use and any upgrades they wish to take as follows:

3.1.1 ENGAGEMENT SCALE

Before building lists, players must agree on the Engagement Scale of the game. This defines the Maximum Mineral Limit for each army and dictates the size of the battlefield required.


  1. Skirmish Level (Up to 1,000 Minerals)

    1. Fast games, learning core mechanics, small elite actions, and tight skirmishes.

    2. Battlefield Size: 36" x 36"


  1. Standard Engagement (Up to 2,000 Minerals)

    1. The definitive StarCraft experience. Balanced play, tournaments, and most narrative missions.

    2. Battlefield Size: 36" x 54"


  1. Grand Offensive (2,001+ Minerals)

    1. Large-scale battles for experienced commanders managing multi-front pressure and heavy assets.

    2. Battlefield Size: 36" x 72"

3.1.2 SELECTING A FACTION

Each player chooses one of the three StarCraft races: Terran, Zerg or Protoss. Once selected, each player must select exactly one Faction Card. Each Faction Card provides the structure of your army, and determines which Units you may bring and what Tactical Cards you may use:
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770399294800_38Aa.JPG?alt=media&token=04bd5c0c-2330-47c1-9b7b-aa23bb38cfb0

1. Faction Tags: These are the Tags a Unit or Tactical Card must have to include them in your army. Some Units and Tactical Cards have multiple Faction Tags.


EXAMPLE

The Kerrigan’s Brood Faction Card has the ZERG and KERRIGAN’S BROOD tags. This means that you can only include Units and Tactical Cards with the ZERG and/or KERRIGAN’S BROOD Faction Tags in your army.


  1. Base Army Slots: The starting Supply Pool of organization slots (e.g. 1x Core, 1x Hero, 1x Support).

3.1.3 RESOURCE ALLOCATION: MINERALS

Players spend Minerals to recruit Units and purchase permanent Unit Upgrades. The total Mineral cost of all your Units and Upgrades must not exceed the agreed Engagement Scale limit. Unspent Minerals are lost; they do not carry over into the game.

3.1.4 RESOURCE ALLOCATION: VESPENE GAS

Each player receives a separate budget of Vespene Gas equal to 10% of the agreed Engagement Scale Limit. Vespene Gas is spent exclusively on Tactical Cards. It cannot be converted into Minerals to recruit more Units. Unspent Vespene Gas is lost.


EXAMPLE

In a game with a 2,000 Engagement Scale limit, you have 200 Vespene Gas to spend.

3.1.5 TACTICAL CARDS AND ARMY SLOTS

Tactical Cards are special army-building tools that allow you to take different types of Units in your army. In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, Units occupy Army Slots (Core, Elite, Support, Air, Hero.) that govern the overall structure and playstyle of your army. Where Faction Cards provide a few basic Army Slots, Tactical Cards are purchased to unlock more slots and expand your forces. 


EXAMPLE

A Barracks card is required to open additional Core slots, allowing the recruitment of Marines Units. A Factory card is needed to open Elite slots for heavy armour.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

Players can invest their Vespene Gas into low-cost tactical cards to open a massive number of CORE Army Slots, ignoring high-cost upgrades. This will allow them to overwhelm their opponent early with cheap massed Units - the tabletop equivalent of a Zergling Rush.

3.1.6 MUSTERING UNITS

Once your available Army Slots are established by your cards, you occupy each slot with eligible Units. Each Unit occupies a specific number of Army Slots based on their Supply Cost, as listed on its Unit Card. You must pay the Mineral cost for every Unit recruited. Unused Army Slots are lost.


EXAMPLE

A small Marauder Unit might occupy 1 CORE Army Slot, while a full-strength Marauder Unit might occupy 2 CORE Army Slots.

3.1.7 PURCHASING UPGRADES

Players may customize Units by purchasing Upgrades listed on the reverse of their Unit Card. If an upgrade lists a cost but no special symbol, the item or ability is added to the Unit’s profile (e.g. Grenades) normally.


If an Upgrade entry includes an Up Arrow (↑) followed by "FOR [Weapon Name]", this is a replacement. The model loses the original weapon profile entirely and gains the new Upgrade weapon profile in its place.




EXAMPLE

"AGG-12 ↑ FOR C-14 Rifle" means the Marine swaps their standard rifle for the AGG-12.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770399365951_42.jpg?alt=media&token=8afbe0e0-17c8-478a-9253-fc085ecad31d

3.1.8 TEAM GAMES

In multiplayer battles (2v2, 3v3, or Free-For-All) the players agree on a Total Mineral Value for each team (e.g. 4,000 Minerals per Team). Teams may divide this budget among its players however they wish (e.g. Player A takes 1,000 Minerals and Player B takes 3,000 Minerals), provided the team total is legal.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

Check page XXX for army roster examples with Deployment and Missions.

3.2 MISSION SELECTION AND THE DRAFT

To ensure fair play (especially in competitive environments) StarCraft: The Miniatures Game uses a Draft System to determine what mission is to be played and how the armies are deployed. This ensures that neither player walks into a scenario rigged by chance or collection size. Each draft follows the same four steps:


  1. THE DRAFT POOL

    1. Each player must bring 2 Mission Cards and 2 Deployment Cards to the table.

    2. A player may not bring two copies of the same card (e.g. you cannot bring two copies of Supply Drop).

    3. It is permitted for both players to bring the same cards as each other.


  1. THE LAYOUT

    1. Reveal: The players place all their Mission Cards and Deployment cards face-up on the table.

    2. Arrange: Organize the cards into two distinct rows:

      1. Row 1: All 4 Mission Cards.

      2. Row 2: All 4 Deployment Cards.


  1. INITIATIVE AND CONTROL

    1. The players Roll-Off (2D6). The winner gains the Initiative and must immediately choose to be the Red Player or the Blue Player. This determines which ENTRY EDGE, Zone of Influence, and Mission Markers (Odd vs. Even) belong to that player for the duration of the game.

    2. The player with the Initiative then chooses to Control either the Mission Draft OR the Deployment Draft. Their opponent automatically gains Control of the remaining Draft.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

Controlling the Mission Draft allows you to secure objectives that suit your army's composition. Controlling the Deployment Draft allows you to dictate the geometry of the battlefield (e.g. picking a battlefield with close deployment zones to favour Units that excel in close combat).


  1. THE DRAFT SEQUENCE

    1. Resolve the Mission Draft first, followed by the Deployment Draft. Resolve each draft in the following order for each row of cards:

      1. The player WITHOUT Control of this draft acts first. They must select and remove 2 Cards from the row. These cards are vetoed and will not be played.

      2. The player WITH Control of this draft selects 1 Card from the remaining 2 to be played. The unpicked card is discarded.

3.2.1 UNDERSTANDING MISSION AND DEPLOYMENT CARDS

  1. MISSION CARDS

Each Mission Card contains:

  1. Timing Windows: Specific rules resolved at moments like "Beginning of the Round" or "Scoring Phase."

  2. Logistics (Supply Cap): Two crucial values that govern the Supply System (see 2.1):

  3. Starting Supply: The Supply Limit available in Round 1.

  4. Escalation: The amount the Supply Limit increases at the start of each subsequent Round.

  5. Game Length: The number of rounds played (usually 5).

  6. Additional Conditions: Some missions allow for an instant win (Sudden Death) if specific criteria are met (e.g. leading by 10 VP).


  1. DEPLOYMENT CARDS

Each Deployment Card contains:

  1. Battlefield Dimensions: The required battlefield size (e.g. 54" x 36").

  2. Marker Coordinates: Precise measurements for placing Mission Markers 1–5.

  3. Entry Edge: The edge that models can be deployed onto the battlefield.

  4. Zone of Influence:The specific area defined by the coordinates on the Deployment Card. This zone extends 6” inward from the player's ENTRY EDGE.


  1. MARKER AFFINITY

Once the Draft is complete and colours are chosen, the Mission Markers on the Deployment Card gain specific affinities based on their number:

  1. Odd Markers (1 & 3): Associated with the Red Player.

  2. Even Markers (2 & 4): Associated with the Blue Player.

  3. Marker 5: Always Neutral.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385292635_21.jpg?alt=media&token=49005e58-e411-40fb-824e-7bcc4215fa5c

3.3 BATTLEFIELD SETUP SEQUENCE

Once the Mission and Deployment have been drafted, set up the battlefield in the following order:


  1. PLACE MISSION MARKERS 

Place Mission Markers using the measurement coordinates shown on the selected Deployment Card. 


  1. PLACE TERRAIN PIECES

Starting with Red Player, the players alternate placing one terrain feature at a time. To ensure fairness, visually divide the battlefield into four quarters. Players should aim to place a similar amount of terrain in each quarter. Leave sufficient room for miniatures to navigate. Terrain pieces cannot be placed directly on top of a Mission Marker.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

As a guideline, keep at least 6" between major structures so larger models are not trapped by layout.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385343326_22.jpg?alt=media&token=11c17e93-29f8-412b-9da3-be28ad6b4d60

3.4 DEPLOYMENT AND RESERVES

In StarCraft: The Miniatures Game, armies do not begin the battle already in formation on the battlefield. Instead, players must manage logistics in real time - bringing forces onto the field as the round unfolds and as Supply allows. At the start of each game, no Units are deployed on the battlefield. All Units begin in Reserves.

3.4.1 THE SUPPLY POOL

Deployment is governed by the Supply Pool. This represents the logistical capacity available to support troops on the frontline. At the start of each Game Round, the player’s Supply Pool is set to the value defined by the Mission Card:


  • Round 1: Equal to the Mission's Starting Supply.

  • Later Rounds: Increases by the Supply Escalation value each round.

  • Hard Cap: The Supply Pool acts as a hard limit. At no point in the game can the Total Current Supply of your Units on the battlefield exceed your Supply Pool.

3.4.2 CALCULATING AVAILABLE SUPPLY

During the Movement Phase, players may order Units from Reserves to enter the battlefield. To do so, they must check their Available Supply:


  • Available Supply = (Current Supply Pool) minus (Total Supply of Friendly Units currently on the Battlefield).

  • Deployment Cost: A Unit may only be deployed if its Current Supply Value is less than or equal to the player's Available Supply.

  • As Units on the battlefield are destroyed (or their Supply drops due to casualties), they free up your Available Supply, allowing new reinforcements to arrive in later rounds.

3.4.3 DEPLOYMENT RESTRICTIONS

When a Unit is Activated to deploy from Reserves, they typically enter via the player's ENTRY EDGE, as defined by the Deployment Card. They perform a Move Action measured from that battlefield edge. Under no circumstances may a Unit that arrived onto the battlefield during the current Game Phase finish its Activation within an opponent’s Zone of Influence. This applies to all forms of arrival, including Moving on from a battlefield edge, disembarking from a Warp Prism, or being Summoned.

4.0 THE GAME SEQUENCE

This section of the core rules covers the individual phases that make up a standard Game Round and the different actions that can be used within them.

4.1 ROUNDS AND PHASES

StarCraft: The Miniatures Game is played over a series of Game Rounds. Unless specified otherwise by the Mission Card, a standard game lasts for 5 Rounds. Each Round is strictly divided into four Phases. Players must resolve each Phase fully before proceeding to the next.

PHASE 1: THE MOVEMENT PHASE

Reinforce, reposition, prepare for the engagement.

Key Actions: Deploy from Reserves, Move, Disengage, Hold.

⬇

PHASE 2: THE ASSAULT PHASE 

Apply firepower, commit charges, break the enemy.

Key Actions: Ranged Attack, Charge, Run, Hold.

⬇

PHASE 3: THE COMBAT PHASE 

Consolidate Units in combat, resolve melee clashes.

Key Actions: Close Ranks, Fight.

⬇

PHASE 4: THE SCORING & CLEANUP PHASE 

Score Victory Points, reset temporary states, determine initiative.

Key Actions: Mission Marker Control, VP Tally, Refresh Cards.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385526393_23.jpg?alt=media&token=9d8f1766-eb8b-4f6a-8005-6562d12b8469

4.2 THE ACTIVATION SYSTEM

During Phases 1, 2 and 3, players do not resolve the actions of all their Units at once. They use an Alternating Activation system.


  • The player holding the First Player Marker decides who Activates the first Unit of the Phase.

  • The player picks a friendly Unit that is already on the battlefield and has not yet been Activated in the given phase.

  • After a Unit has finished its action, play passes to the opponent. Players continue to take turns, alternating one-by-one, until the phase ends.

  • When a Unit is Activated, it performs exactly one Action from the list available in that Phase.

4.2.1 PASSING & INITIATIVE  

A player may choose to Pass instead of activating a Unit (or must Pass if they have no eligible Units left) when it is their turn to do so.


Seizing the Initiative: The first player to Pass in a Phase immediately claims the First Player Marker for the next Phase. Once a player Passes, they cannot Activate any more Units in the current Phase. The opponent may continue activating their remaining Units until they also Pass.

4.2.2 DETERMINING THE FIRST PLAYER

Players roll off (see xxx). The winner chooses which player takes the First Player Marker for the first Game Round.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

Passing early in the Movement Phase allows you to see your opponent’s move before reacting. But more importantly, it guarantees you the first activation in the following Assault Phase.

4.3 PHASE 1: THE MOVEMENT PHASE

During the Movement Phase, Units on the battlefield reposition and Reserves enter the fray. Before any actions are taken, follow these steps:


RESOLVE START OF THE ROUND EFFECTS

  • Resolve any rules, abilities, or Tactical Cards that trigger at the "Start of the Round".

  • If both players have effects, the holder of the First Player Marker resolves theirs first.


CHECK SUPPLY

All players must verify the current Available Supply. This determines which Units currently in Reserves are eligible to be deployed during this Phase. Remember, Available Supply = (Current Supply Pool) minus (Total Supply of Friendly Units on the Battlefield).

4.3.1 ACTIVATIONS

Players take turns activating one Unit at a time, starting with the player chosen by the holder of the First Player Marker. On a player’s turn, they must do one of the following:


  • Activate a Unit without an Activation Marker on the Battlefield: Perform a Move, Hold, or Disengage action.

  • Activate a Unit without an Activation Marker in Reserves: Perform a Move action to enter the battlefield.


Once a Unit finishes its action, place an Activation Marker (Movement Side) next to it.

4.3.2 PASSING 

The first player to Pass claims the First Player Marker for the next Phase. Once a player Passes, they cannot Activate any more Units in the current Phase.


Mark Remaining Units

Immediately place an Activation Marker (Movement Side) next to each of your Units on the battlefield that did not Activate. These Units count as having performed a Hold action for the remainder of the phase.

4.4 MOVEMENT PHASE ACTIONS

4.4.1 HOLD

  • It is considered Activated for the phase. 

  • Place an Activation Marker as normal.

4.4.2 MOVE

The Move Action is split into two steps depending on the number of models in the acting Unit. If the acting Unit has only 1 model, ignore Step 2.

4.4.2 MOVE The Move Action allows the non-Engaged unit to change its position on the battlefield. Only a Unit that is currently Non-Engaged may declare this action. Resolve a Move. Only a Unit that is currently Non-Engaged may declare this action. Resolve a Move. 4.4.3 MOVING A UNIT The Move is split into two steps depending on the number of models in the acting Unit. If the acting Unit has only 1 model, ignore Step 2.



STEP 1 - THE LEADING MODEL

  • Choose one model in the Unit to be the Leading Model for this action. 

  • Move only the Leading Model a distance up to the Unit’s Speed. 

  • Measure the actual path traveled. The distance cannot exceed Speed. 

  • The Leading Model may pass through friendly models in its own Unit. 

  • The Leading Model cannot move through enemy models or friendly models from other Units. The base must physically fit through any gap. 

  • The Leading Model cannot end within Engagement Range (1") of an enemy Unit.

  • The Leading Model cannot move through terrain of Size 2 or larger.

  • The Leading Model cannot change their elevation unless it moves through an ACCESS POINT connecting two different elevations. In that case, it must end on the elevation it entered after moving through the ACCESS POINT. 



STEP - 2 OTHER MODELS IN THE UNIT

  • Once the Leading Model is placed, pick up all other models in the Unit and Place them on the battlefield.

  • Models must be placed in Unit Coherency (Section 1.6.4).

  • These models ignore intervening obstacles (they are "placed," not moved). However, they must end in a legal position (not overlapping bases or within Engagement Range of an enemy Unit).
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385691765_24.jpg?alt=media&token=cfc7a1e6-b1d0-45bb-a4f5-cf2d7c3c1e4c

SIZE 0 AND SIZE 1 TERRAIN

Unless stated otherwise, models move through Size 0 and Size 1 terrain freely, but cannot end that move overlapping the terrain’s footprint.


GRASS (SIZE 1)

This type of terrain does not block movement. Models can end movement on this type of terrain and it can be removed for the purpose of placing models. Once the models change their position, place the grass back into its original position.

SIZE 2 AND LARGER TERRAIN

  •  Models cannot move through terrain of Size 2 or larger.

ELEVATION

Models can be on one of 3 types of elevation:

  • Models standing directly on the Playmat are on the GROUND LEVEL. 

  • Models standing on horizontal terrain of size 1 or 2 are on MID GROUND.

  • Models standing on horizontal terrain of size 3 or larger are on the HIGH GROUND.

ACCESS POINT

Part of a Terrain that connects different elevation levels. Models moving through an ACCESS POINT can change their elevation.


EXAMPLE OF ACCESS POINT

  • LADDER

  • PORTALS

  • DOORS


EXAMPLE OF MID GROUND TERRAIN

  • RAMP

  • CRATES

  • CONTAINERS


RAMPS

Ramps are a special type of Size 1 MID GROUND terrain that models cannot move through freely. Models may only enter and exit a ramp through its ACCESS POINTS, and may end their movement on the ramp.


Each ramp has two ACCESS POINTS:

  • The edge connecting the two lower corners of the ramp.

  • The edge connecting the two upper corners of the ramp.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770385781609_26.jpg?alt=media&token=4d227474-749b-43f6-a8f8-07f7496bcce5

FLYING MOVE

Units with the FLYING keyword ignore the normal restrictions for moving. The Leading Model moves directly point-to-point, ignoring all terrain (vertical or horizontal) and all models between the start and end points. Friendly and Enemy Leading Models may move through a FLYING model's base as if it were not there.

4.4.3 DISENGAGE

The Disengage Action allows a Unit within the Engagement Range of an enemy Unit to retreat.


  • Only a Unit that is currently Engaged may declare this action. Resolve a Move Action.

  • Any model in the Unit that cannot end its movement strictly outside the Engagement Range of all enemy Units is immediately removed from the battlefield and considered destroyed.

  • A Unit that Disengages cannot declare a Ranged Attack or Charge in the subsequent Assault Phase, unless the Disengaging Unit’s Current Supply is greater than the Combined Supply of all enemies it was Engaged with.

4.4.4 DEPLOY 

To Deploy a Unit during the Movement Phase:


  • Ensure the Unit's Supply Cost fits within your Available Supply.

  • The Unit performs a Move action starting from the player's ENTRY EDGE.

  • Measure the move from the battlefield edge.

  • The Leading Model enters first. The rest of the Unit is placed in Coherency (Section 1.6.4).

  • The Unit cannot finish its Move Action within the enemy's Zone of Influence.

  • The Unit being Deployed cannot use any Special Abilities (Section 5.1)


UNITS RETURNED TO RESERVES 

Some rules may remove a Unit from the battlefield and place it back into Reserves. When this happens, the Unit is not destroyed - it has simply left the battlefield.


  • When a Unit is returned to Reserves, it retains any Markers or Tokens currently affecting it. These items must be placed alongside the stored models to ensure they remain clearly associated with that Unit while it is off the battlefield.

  • A Unit that is returned to Reserves keeps all Damage it has suffered earlier in the game. When that Unit is deployed back onto the battlefield, it returns with the same Damage already allocated to it.

  • If a Unit is returned to the battlefield, it keeps all other effects that were applied to it before it left the battlefield (such as BUFFs, deBUFFs, status effects and mission effects), provided those effects have not expired.

  • If the Unit was already Activated in the current Phase when it was removed, it remains Activated if it returns in that same Phase - it cannot be Activated again in that Phase.

4.5 PHASE 2: THE ASSAULT PHASE

During the Assault Phase, Units can open fire on the enemy, sacrifice firepower for additional positioning or charge at the enemy.

4.5.1 ACTIVATIONS

Players take turns activating one Unit at a time, starting with the player chosen by the holder of the First Player Marker. On a player’s turn, they must do one of the following: 


  • Activate a Unit with an Activation Marker on the Movement Side on the Battlefield: Perform a Run, Hold, Ranged Attack or Charge action.


Once a Unit finishes its action, place an Activation Marker (Assault Side) next to it.


4.5.2 PASSING 

The first player to Pass claims the First Player Marker for the next Phase. Once a player Passes, they cannot Activate any more Units in the current Phase.


Mark Remaining Units

Immediately place an Activation Marker (Assault Side) next to each of your Units on the battlefield that did not Activate. These Units count as having performed a Hold action for the remainder of the phase.

4.6 ASSAULT PHASE ACTIONS

4.6.1 RUN

  • Pick a friendly Unit that is not Engaged.

  • It Moves using the standard Move Action rules (Section 4.4.2).

4.6.2 HOLD

  • It is considered Activated for the phase. 

  • Place an Activation Marker as normal.

4.6.3 RANGED ATTACK

The Ranged Attack action is the primary method of inflicting damage in this phase. To resolve this action, follow these steps:


  1. TARGET ELIGIBILITY

To be a valid target for a specific Batch, the enemy Unit must meet all of the following criteria relative to the attacking models:


  • The Visible models from the target Unit must be within the weapon’s maximum Range.

  • Measure from the edge of the attacking model’s base to the edge of any Visible model’s base in the target Unit.

  • Only the specific models that are within Range and with Visible target contribute dice to the Attack Roll.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770399529165_39a.JPG?alt=media&token=31386be9-a03f-48ba-8503-e8b2b2f02803

TARGET TYPE

The target must match the weapon’s Target characteristic:

  • GROUND: Can only target Units with the GROUND tag.

  • FLYING: Can only target Units with the FLYING tag.

  • ALL: Can target any Unit.


ENGAGEMENT STATUS

Engaged Units cannot be selected as targets. However, they may be hit by Spillover from Template Weapons if they are covered by the template.


If the attacking Unit is currently Engaged, it may make a Ranged Attack against the enemy Unit(s) it is Engaged with. It cannot fire out of combat at distant targets.


  1. WEAPON SELECTION AND BATCHING

When a Unit makes a Ranged Attack, each model in that Unit attacks with one of its available weapons. Only weapons assigned to the Assault Phase on the Unit Card are eligible.


TARGET LOCK

All models in the Unit using the same Weapon Profile (e.g. C-14 Rifle) must target the same enemy Unit. You cannot split the fire of a single Weapon Profile  across multiple targets.


SPLITTING FIRE

If a Unit is equipped with different Weapon Profiles (e.g. 5 Marines with C-14 Rifles and 1 Marine with a Rocket Launcher), the Unit may target different enemy Units. You must resolve these attacks in separate Batches.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770399693992_43a.JPG?alt=media&token=069126a7-b698-44c7-8970-8e2c17cf5719

BATCHES

A Batch consists of all weapons of the same Weapon Profile firing at the same target Unit. Each individual Batch is resolved before the target of the next Batch is declared, you do not have to declare the targets of each Batch all at once:  


  • Declare the target of a Batch. 

  • Resolve the Attacks for that Batch only.

  • Declare the target of the next Batch.

  • Resolve the Attacks for that Batch only.

And so on.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

If a friendly Unit is Engaged with an enemy Unit, and a Batch resolved for the friendly Unit destroys the enemy Unit it is Engaged with, the friendly Unit immediately becomes Unengaged (unless it is Engaged with a different Enemy Unit). Any remaining Batches may select targets as normal.

4.6.4 RANGED ATTACK RESOLUTION

Repeat the following sequence for each Batch of Ranged Attacks made by a Unit:


  1. ROLL TO HIT & SURGE

  • For each attacking model in the batch with an eligible target, add a number of Attack Dice equal to that weapon’s Rate of Attack (RoA).

  • If the weapon has an S Dice characteristic (e.g. D3, D6), roll that die along with the Attack Dice (use different coloured dice). If it has no S Dice, the Surge Result is 0.

  • Roll to Hit: Roll the Attack Dice. Any result equal to or greater than the weapon’s Hit value is a success.

  • Move all successful Hit dice into the Armour Pool.


DESIGNER’S NOTE

If the dice in a Batch has different modifiers, roll them separately. It still counts as the same Batch.


  1. RESOLVE SURGE

Surge represents weapons designed to penetrate specific defenses:


  • Check if the target Unit’s Combat Tag (e.g. Light, Armoured) matches the weapon’s Surge Type. If it does not match, the Surge result is ignored.

  • If it matches, move a number of dice equal to the Surge Result from the Armour Pool directly to the Damage Pool. These dice bypass the Armour Roll entirely. Surge can never move more dice than are currently in the Armour Pool.


  1. DEFENSE ROLLS

  • The defending player takes all dice currently in the Armour Pool and rolls them.

  • Any result equal to or higher than the Unit’s Armour characteristic is a success. Discard the die.

  • Any result lower than the Unit’s Armour characteristic is a failure. Move the die to the Damage Pool.

1. EVADE ROLLS

This step is performed only if the target Unit meets a specific condition that grants an Evade Roll:


  • A Special Ability or keyword that grants an Evade roll attempt.

  • The target Unit is Engaged, and Damage is being applied by a Ranged Attack.

  • If eligible, the defending player takes the dice currently in the Damage Pool and rolls them.

  • Any result equal to or higher than the Unit’s Evade characteristic is a Success. Remove those dice from the pool.

  • Any dice that remain in the Damage Pool are confirmed hits. Resolve them as damage in the next step.

1. RESOLVE DAMAGE & CASUALTIES

  • Count the number of dice remaining in the Damage Pool. Multiply that number by the attacking weapon’s Damage characteristic.

  • If the Unit already has a Damage marker (a dice for example), add its current value to this new damage. This sum is the Unit’s Total Damage.

  • Resolve any Special Skills that can reduce Total Damage at this step.


  • Damage is allocated to the Unit as a whole, but casualties are removed model by model:

    • When resolving a Ranged Attack, you cannot remove more models than the number of Eligible models in the target Unit (models the attacker has Line of Sight to).

    • As long as the Total Damage is equal to or higher than a model's Hit Points (HP), the defending player must remove one Visible model and reduce the Total Damage by that model's HP. Repeat this process until the remaining damage is less than the model's HP or no Visible models remain.

    • If all Visible models are destroyed, any remaining Total Damage is discarded. It does not carry over to non-Visible models.

If any Total Damage remains but is insufficient to remove the next Visible model, record it by placing or updating a Damage marker (a D6 dice for example) next to the Unit.

4.6.5 REMOVING MODELS FROM ENGAGED UNITS

When targeting an enemy Unit that is Engaged, standard Visibility rules for casualty removal are ignored. Instead, casualties from must be removed by the Defending Player in the following strict order of Priority:


Priority 1: Remove models that are not within the Engagement Range of any enemy Unit first.

Priority 2: Remove models that are within Engagement Range but not in Base-to-Base contact next.

Priority 3: Remove models that are physically touching (Base-to-Base) with enemy models last.


When selecting a model to remove from Priority 2 or 3, the Defending Player cannot select a model if removing it would cause the Unit to break contact (cease being Engaged) with a specific enemy Unit, provided there is another valid casualty available in any priority step.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386138559_29.jpg?alt=media&token=28435351-20a7-4cdb-8349-dab92852318d

4.6.6 TEMPLATE WEAPONS

Weapons with the TEMPLATE keyword affect an area rather than a specific model. Use the following steps:


  1. ALIGNMENT

  • Blast Template (BT): The center hole must be over the target model’s base (Wholly Within if possible).

  • Flamer Template (FT): The narrow end must be flush with the attacking model’s base, aimed at the target.

  • Templates only affect models on the same vertical level (GROUND LEVEL, RAMP LEVEL, or HIGH GROUND) as the declared target.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386180811_31.jpg?alt=media&token=64e77ba7-2188-4bf7-a1fc-2a8cb82c6743

1. DETERMINING HITS

  • Any model (Friendly or Enemy) whose base is partially or fully covered is Affected.

  • Trace a line from the center hole of the Blast Template or attacker's base when Units  Flamer Template to the target. It is blocked by Terrain Size 2 or higher.


  1. ATTACK POOL GENERATION

Unlike standard attacks, Template Weapons determine their dice count based on the number of targets caught in the blast. Generate a number of Attack Dice equal to:


  • The number of models in the target Unit covered by the template.

  • Plus the weapon’s Rate of Attack (RoA) modifiers (if any).


  • Spillover: If models from other Units (friend or foe) are covered, resolve them as separate batches.

    • Attack Dice equal the number of models covered in that Unit.

    • Ignore RoA modifiers for Spillover attacks.


  1. RESOLUTION SEQUENCE

  • Roll the generated Attack Dice for the batch.

  • Any result equal to or higher than the weapon’s Hit characteristic is a hit. Move these dice to the Armour Pool.

  • Any result lower than the Hit characteristic is a miss.

  • If a Template Weapon has a Surge Type, do not roll a Surge Die. Instead:

    • The Surge Result is equal to the total number of models affected in the current batch.

    • Apply Surge efficiency as normal if the Target's Combat Tag matches the Weapon's Surge Type.

4.6.7 CHARGE

The Charge action allows an Non-Engaged Unit to enter the Engagement Range of enemy Units. To resolve this action, follow these steps:


  1. DECLARE TARGETS

The Active Player selects the acting Non-Engaged Unit and declares one or more enemy Units to be the targets of the Charge:


  • The targets must share the same Tag Type (GROUND vs. GROUND, or FLYING vs. FLYING).

  • Unlike shooting, a Charge does not require the target to be Visible (Line of Sight). You may charge enemies hidden behind walls or ruins.

  • If you intend to engage multiple enemy Units at once, you must declare all of them as targets before rolling any dice.


  1. DETERMINE CHARGE DISTANCE

  • Select one model in the charging Unit to be the Leading Model. For each target Unit, nominate one enemy model as the Charge Target.

  • Roll 1D6 and add the result to the Unit’s Speed characteristic.

  • The result is the Charge Roll Distance. It represents the maximum distance the Leading Model can move during this action.

  • This distance is measured along the actual path of travel. If the model must move around a wall, the distance is measured around the wall, not through it.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386235589_32.jpg?alt=media&token=8e13f8d8-19d1-4b75-82de-28ebd4c4d95b

1. SUCCESSFUL AND FAILED CHARGES

A Charge is Successful if and only if the Leading Model can be placed legally in a position that meets all of the following criteria:


  • The position must be Wholly Within the Charge Roll Distance of its starting point.

  • The position is within Engagement Range (1") of the declared Charge Target(s).

  • The model’s base fits physically in that location without overlapping other bases or impassable terrain.

  • The model cannot end within Engagement Range (1") of a unit that was not declared as a Charge Target(s).


If multiple Charge targets were declared, the Charge is only successful if the Leading Model can be placed within the Engagement Range of each target that was declared.


FAILED CHARGES

If the Leading Model cannot be moved into a valid position (e.g. the roll was too low or there is no physical space to fit), the Charge Fails. The Unit does not move. It remains in its original position and its activation ends immediately.


  1. EXECUTE THE MOVE

If the Charge is Successful, move the Leading Model into the valid position identified in Step 3. You must attempt to place the Leading Model in Base-to-Base Contact with the Charge Target(s) if possible. If not, place it as close as possible within the Engagement Range of the target.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386306770_33.jpg?alt=media&token=a367cbf4-3b3c-482e-a9bd-771dbd6487c7

OTHER MODELS IN THE UNIT

Pick up all remaining models in the charging Unit and place them in Coherency, so none of the models are within an Engagement Range (1") of a unit that was not declared as a Charge Target(s).  


 You must follow this strict placement priority:


  • Priority A (Base-to-Base): You must place models in Base-to-Base contact with enemy models from the declared target Unit(s) whenever physically possible.

  • Priority B (Engagement): If Base-to-Base is impossible, place models within Engagement Range of the target(s).

  • Priority C (Coherency): If neither is possible, place the remaining models as close as possible to the Leading Model to maintain Coherency.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386348796_34.jpg?alt=media&token=bc1b1f05-237b-450e-bb9b-6b49fbdc26a0

4.7 PHASE 3: COMBAT PHASE

During the Combat Phase, Engaged Units move to close the distance to their foes and trade blows in brutal melee combat. Unlike the previous phases where activation is voluntary, all Units currently in Engagement Range must act in this Phase. You cannot choose to skip a fight. 


ACTIVATIONS

Players take turns activating one Engaged Unit at a time, starting with the player chosen by the holder of the First Player Marker. When an eligible Unit is Activated, it follows the Close Combat Action Sequence below.


UNIT ELIGIBILITY 

Only Engaged Units are allowed to act in this Phase. Once a Unit’s combat is resolved, remove its Activation Marker.


PASSING

A player only Passes when they have no Engaged Units remaining to Activate. When both players Pass, the phase ends.

4.7.1 THE CLOSE COMBAT ACTION SEQUENCE

  1. CLOSE RANKS

This step is optional. Before rolling any dice, the Unit acts to surround the enemy and bring more weapons to bear:


  • The controlling player selects a Leading Model and moves it up to 3". This move follows Movement rules (Section 4.4.2) but has two specific restrictions:

    • The Leading Model may only move if it ends the move closer to the Unit(s) it is Engaged with.

    • This move cannot cause the Unit to become Engaged with any new enemy Units that were not already Engaged at the start of this Phase.


  • Reposition the rest of the Unit into Coherency with the Leading Model.

  • Models must be placed in Base-to-Base contact with enemy models whenever possible.

  • If Base-to-Base contact is not possible, place as many models as possible in Base-to-Base contact with friendly models from their own Unit that are within Engagement Range of the enemy.

  • Models that are already in Base-to-Base contact at the start of this step are not moved. They are physically pinned by the enemy and cannot be placed any closer.

  • Models from the Unit making this move cannot Disengage from other enemy units.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386421732_35.jpg?alt=media&token=f5315854-c3c3-44a8-b031-f5949a8d4859

1. DECLARE ATTACKERS

Determine which models in the Unit are eligible to strike. A model may attack if it occupies one of two "Ranks":


  • The Fighting Rank: The model is within Engagement Range (1") of an enemy model.

  • The Supporting Rank: The model is in Base-to-Base contact with a friendly model from its own Unit that is in the Fighting Rank.


Line of Sight is not required. If a model is eligible to attack, it strikes the enemy Unit as a whole.
https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/starcrafttmgbeta.firebasestorage.app/o/rules_images%2F1770386467747_36.jpg?alt=media&token=60eeeb77-7d5a-455b-a80a-b257297f12eb

1. ROLL ATTACKS & SURGE

Attacks are resolved using the standard attack sequence for Ranged Attacks (Section 4.6.4) with the following restrictions:


  • Every weapon assigned to the Combat Phase on the Unit Card is treated as a Close Combat Weapon.

  • If a model has more than one eligible weapon, the controlling player must choose one weapon for that model to use.


FIGHTING MULTIPLE UNITS

If the Unit is Engaged with multiple enemy Units simultaneously:


  • For models that are in Fighting/Supporting Ranks with more than one enemy Unit, the controlling player declares how they split their Attack Dice between eligible targets.

  • If fighting multiple enemies, the controlling player must declare which specific Attack Pool (targeting which enemy Unit) receives the Surge Die before rolling. The Surge Die cannot be split.


  1. CASUALTY REMOVAL

Casualties in Combat Phase are removed by the Defending Player using the rules for removing models from engaged units (Section 4.6.5). 


  1. POST-COMBAT STATUS

After casualties are removed, check the status of the surviving Units:

  1. If, after removing casualties, none of the Unit’s remaining models are within 1" of an enemy, the Unit is immediately considered Unengaged.

  2. If a Unit becomes Unengaged because the enemy it was fighting was destroyed:

  3. If it has not yet Activated this Phase, it is no longer bound by the mandatory Combat activation rules. It effectively "Passes" its turn to fight, but it is free to act normally in the next Round.

  4. In rare cases (via Reaction abilities or specific triggers), a Unit freed from combat might be able to perform actions if a rule allows it.

title: 4.0 THE GAME SEQUENCE - SCORING AND CLEANUP PHASE

content.value:
4.8 PHASE 4: THE SCORING & CLEANUP PHASE

During the Scoring & Cleanup Phase, the players determine which Units control Mission Markers, if any special Mission Objectives have been completed and refresh the battlefield for the next Game Round. The player holding the First Player Marker initiates the steps in the following order:


  1. Determine Mission Marker Control

  2. Score Victory Points

  3. End of Game Check

  4. Resolve END OF THE ROUND Rules

  5. Cleanup & Refresh

  6. Determine Initiative


4.8.1 DETERMINE MISSION MARKER CONTROL

In StarCraft, Control is determined by Supply, not model count. Follow these steps:


  1. IDENTIFY CONTESTING UNITS

A Unit is eligible to Contest a Mission Marker only if it meets all three of the following criteria:

  • It is currently on the battlefield (not in Reserves).

  • At least one of its models is Within 3" of the Mission Marker.

  • The Unit is In Coherency (Abators’ Call: Remember, Units that are Out of Coherency cannot control Mission Markers).


  1. CALCULATE CONTROL

For each Mission Marker:

  • Each player calculates the Total Current Supply Value of all their eligible contesting Units.

  • The player with the higher Supply Value Controls the marker.

  • If both player’s Total Current Supply Value is tied, the marker is Contested.

  • A Unit reduced to Supply 0 can still Control a marker if there are no enemy models contesting it.


  1. STICKY CONTROL

  • If a player Controls a marker, they place a Faction Indicator on the marker to signify control.

  • Once controlled, a marker remains under that player’s Control (even if they move all their Units away) until it is explicitly reclaimed by the opponent. 

  • If a marker becomes Contested (tied score), Control does not change:

    • If previously controlled, it remains with the current owner.

    • If previously uncontrolled, it remains neutral.


4.8.2 SCORING VICTORY POINTS (VPs)

  • Both players simultaneously tally their VPs for the round.

  • Consult the Mission Card and Mission Rules for specific scoring conditions (e.g. "Gain 2 VPs for each Controlled Marker associated with the Opponent’s colour.").


4.8.3 END OF GAME CHECK

Before refreshing the battlefield, check if the game ends immediately. The game ends if:

  • The Special Winning Condition has been fulfilled, the game ends immediately and the player who met the criteria automatically wins the game.

  • A player has no models remaining on the battlefield or in Reserves. If the game ends due to Total Elimination, the surviving player gains +10 VP.

  • The final Phase of the final Game Round (usually Round 5) has been completed (check 4.9 The Final Score).



If the game ends, proceed directly to Section 4.8.1 Victory Conditions. Otherwise, proceed to Resolve END OF ROUND Effects.


4.8.4 RESOLVE END OF ROUND EFFECTS

  • Resolve any rules, abilities, or Tactical Cards that trigger "At the End of the Round".

  • If both players have effects, the holder of the First Player Marker resolves theirs first.


4.8.5 CLEANUP & REFRESH

  • Remove all Tokens and Markers from the battlefield:

    • Do not remove Tokens with the STAY IN PLAY keyword.

    • Do not remove Damage Markers.

    • Do not remove Mission Markers

    • Do not remove Faction Indicators on Mission Markers.


  • Flip all Exhausted Tactical and Faction Cards back to their active (face-up) side. They are now ready to generate resources (CP, BM, PE) for the next round.


4.8.6 DETERMINE INITIATIVE 

  • Check the current total Victory Points.

  • The First Player Marker is awarded to the player with fewer Victory Points than their opponent.

  • If the score is tied, perform a Roll-Off. The winner takes the First Player Marker.

  • Proceed to Phase 1: The Movement Phase of the next Game Round.

4.9 The Final Score


Calculating the Final Score: If the game has ended due to the Round Limit only, calculate the winner using the following steps:

  1. Mission Score: Total the Victory Points earned throughout the game from Mission objectives.

  2. Reveal Reserves: Each player reveals all Units currently in their Reserves (including Units never deployed and Units returned to Reserves).

  3. Reserves Penalty: For each Unit remaining in a player's Reserves, their Opponent gains +10 VP plus an additional +1 VP per point of that Unit's Current Supply.

  4. Determine Winner: The player with the highest final VP total is the winner.

Tiebreaker: If the final score is tied, refer to the Mission Card's tiebreaker. If none exists, the game is a Draw.

5.1 SPECIAL ABILTIES

  • All Special Abilities follow the same universal rules for use and resolution.

  • A Special Ability is always written using a consistent structure to ensure clarity:

    • Name: The ability’s official designation.

    • Phase Limitations: If stated, the ability can only be used during that specific phase.

    • Type: Every ability falls into one of three categories: Passive, Active, or Reaction.

    • Cost: Some abilities require a resource payment (CP, BM, or PE) before resolution.

    • Instruction: The rules text describing exactly what occurs.


GENERIC REQUIREMENTS

An ability can only be used if all requirements in its description are met. This includes:

  • Correct Timing or Trigger.

  • Satisfying any Phase restrictions.

  • Target being within Range and Line of Sight (unless explicitly stated otherwise).

  • Paying any required Costs in full.

  • If even one requirement cannot be met, the ability cannot be declared.

  • The unit must be on the battlefield.

5.2 TYPES OF ABILITIES

ACTIVE ABILITIES

Active Abilities represent manoeuvres, technologies (like Stimpacks), or enhancements that require a deliberate command to execute.


  • An Active Ability is generally used while a friendly Unit is currently Activated.

  • It may be triggered immediately before declaring an Action, or immediately after an Action has been fully resolved. It cannot be used during an Action.

  • If the ability is on a Unit, it cannot be used while that Unit is in Reserves. The Unit must be deployed on the battlefield to use it.

  • Unless stated otherwise, modifiers and effects granted by an Active Ability expire at the End of the Current Round.


LIMITATIONS

Once Per Round: A specific Unit may use a named Active Ability only once per Game Round.

This limit applies individually to each Unit. Two different Marine squads may both use Stimpack in the same round, but a single squad cannot use it twice.

5.2.1 PASSIVE ABILITIES 

Passive Abilities represent innate traits, permanent equipment, or constant biological adaptations:


  • Passive Abilities are always active as long as the Unit is on the battlefield.

  • They do not require the Unit to be Activated to function.

  • Like Active abilities, Passive abilities on a Unit are not in effect while that Unit is in Reserves, unless explicitly stated otherwise.

5.2.2 REACTION ABILITIES

Reaction Abilities allow a player to act outside the normal sequence, responding to specific events:


  • Trigger: Used in direct response to a defined event (e.g. "When this Unit is targeted by an attack").

  • A Reaction must be declared at the exact moment of the trigger. If the window is missed, the ability cannot be used.

  • A Reaction cannot be used while the Unit is in Reserves unless stated otherwise.

  • A player may resolve only one Reaction per each Activation. 


EXAMPLE

If a Zealot Unit is attacked, it may use Zealous Round OR Ground Armor, but not both in the same enemy attack action.


  • If both players wish to use a Reaction to the same trigger:

    • The Active Player (whose turn it currently is) resolves their Reaction first.

    • The Opponent resolves their Reaction second.


NO INTERRUPTION

A player cannot use any Special Ability while an Action is currently in progress (e.g. between a Hit roll and an Armour roll, or while a model is physically moving across the battlefield), unless the ability is explicitly a Reaction designed to interrupt that specific sequence.

LIMITATIONS

Once Per Round: A specific Unit may use a named Reaction Ability only once per Game Round.

This limit applies individually to each Unit. Two different Medic squads may both use Life Support in the same round, but a single squad cannot use it twice.

5.3 TACTICAL CARDS & FACTION CARDS

Tactical Cards and Faction Cards represent a faction’s doctrine, logistics, adaptations, and battlefield tricks. Each card contains specific elements defining its use:
